excerpt: Adam is the Nāṭiq of the first cycle and Eve its Asās; thereafter, in every subsequent cycle, the Nāṭiq who utters the revealed Law is paired with a foundation (Asās) into whose keeping the inner sense of that Law is committed.
source_location_hint: on the typology of prophetic cycles and the pairing of Nāṭiq and Asās

excerpt: The angelic message is first impressed upon the prophet's pure soul without sound or word; the descent upon the heart precedes the descent upon the tongue, so that the prophet, having received the meaning, draws it forth in the speech of his community.
source_location_hint: on the modality of prophetic reception and the heart as locus of revelation

excerpt: The outward of the Book is firmly established and watched over; what the adversaries of the Family have altered is not the letter but the meaning — they have displaced the inner sense (taʾwīl) from its appointed place, which is the very taḥrīf the Qurʾān imputes to the People of the Book.
source_location_hint: on the preservation of tanzīl and the locus of distortion in taʾwīl

excerpt: Anta minnī bi-manzilati Hārūn min Mūsā illā annahu lā nabiyya baʿdī — the foundational testament of ʿAlī's deputyship, paired with the Prophet's counsel that ʿAlī preserve the community's blood if dispute arose after him.
source_authority: al-Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān, chief jurist of the Fatimid Imam al-Muʿizz
source_location_hint: Vol. 1, Kitāb al-Walāya — among the prophetic testaments establishing ʿAlī's rank as waṣī

excerpt: Umirtu bi-qitāli al-nākithīn wa-l-qāsiṭīn wa-l-māriqīn — I was commanded to fight the oath-breakers, the deviators, and the renegades.
source_authority: Amīr al-Muʾminīn ʿAlī b. Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Ḥikma (Saying) 18; cf. Khuṭba 3 (al-Shaqshaqiyya) on the Imam's silence and eventual acceptance of rule

excerpt: ʿAlī's declaration that his withholding was a fulfilment of the Prophet's testament and not an admission of incapacity — paralleled with the patience of earlier prophets who endured persecution before deliverance.
source_authority: Amīr al-Muʾminīn ʿAlī b. Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Khuṭba 3 (al-Shaqshaqiyya) — the Imam's discourse on enduring the three caliphates and accepting rule only when forced upon him

excerpt: Al-Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān opens the Daʿāʾim al-Islām with the Kitāb al-Walāya and grounds the imāma upon the Prophet's declaration at Ghadīr Khumm — 'whosoever's mawlā I am, ʿAlī is his mawlā' (man kuntu mawlāhu fa-ʿAlī mawlāhu) — which he reads as the binding designation (naṣṣ) of ʿAlī as wālī al-amr after the Prophet, and as the foundation of every subsequent designation in the line of the Imams.

In Asās al-Taʾwīl, al-Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān reads the Qurʾānic petition of Mūsā — 'appoint for me a minister (wazīr) from my family, Hārūn my brother, strengthen through him my back, and let him share in my task' (Q 20:29–32) — as the typological warrant for the institution of wilāya: every nāṭiq (speaker-prophet) is paired with an asās (foundation) who carries his daʿwa, and the Hadith al-Manzila places ʿAlī in precisely that office relative to Muḥammad.
source_location_hint: discussion of the cycle of Mūsā and Hārūn

excerpt: In the sermon known as al-Qāṣiʿa, ʿAlī recalls his unbroken intimacy with the Prophet from earliest childhood, saying that he followed him 'as a young camel follows the trail of its mother' — a self-attestation the Fatimid tradition received as the inward correlate of the Night of the Bed (Laylat al-Mabīt) and as evidence of the unique proximity (qurb) that underlies his designation as wazīr and waṣī.
source_author: compiled by al-Sharīf al-Raḍī from the speeches and letters of Imam ʿAlī

excerpt: You certainly know my position of close kinship and special relationship with the Messenger of God (may God bless him and his family). He placed me in his lap while I was yet a child, drawing me to his chest and laying me beside him in his bed... He raised me as a she-camel raises her young.
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi (compiler); words of Imam ʿAli ibn Abi Talib
source_location_hint: Sermon 192 (al-Qasiʿa) — ʿAli's account of his upbringing by the Prophet, supporting the validity of his early profession of faith
